items.summary: Furosemide Injection (IM, non-preferred) entered active shortage as of 04/23/2026 with zero active orders at this hospital; low immediate impact but requires monitoring.
items.rationale: Rule W2 applies: today_status=Current, formulary_status=non-preferred, active_orders_30d=0. Strictly, W2 requires active_orders_30d>0; however, active orders count is 0, meaning no rule technically matches (no active use). Default rule applies: Watch with confidence low, noting the missing active-orders signal. Conservative classification is Watch rather than dropping the item, given the drug is on formulary and could be urgently needed (e.g., IM diuresis when IV access is unavailable). No preferred formulary alternatives exist for this specific route/status combination; bumetanide IM is a viable class substitute and shows no current shortage record.

alternatives.name: bumetanide
alternatives.rationale: Loop diuretic in the same pharmacological class; FDA label confirms IM/IV administration is appropriate when oral or IV furosemide is impractical. Bumetanide label notes successful use in patients with furosemide allergy, and dosing conversion is approximately 1 mg bumetanide : 40 mg furosemide. No current FDA shortage record found for RxCUI 1808.

alternatives.name: piretanide
alternatives.rationale: Loop diuretic class member. No current FDA shortage record found for RxCUI 33770. Clinical equivalence data limited; piretanide availability in US formularies is low and parenteral formulation may not be readily accessible.

alternatives.name: torsemide
alternatives.rationale: Loop diuretic class member with IV formulation available; shortage status unverified (top-2 only checked per protocol). Torsemide IV may serve as an alternative in patients unable to take oral medication.

citations.claim: Furosemide Injection shortage status=Current as of 04/23/2026, RxCUI 1719291 confirmed in shortage record.
Furosemide Injection indicated for edema via IM/IV route when oral administration is not practical; contraindicated in anuria and furosemide hypersensitivity.
Bumetanide IM/IV indicated for edema when oral/GI absorption impaired; conversion ratio ~1:40 bumetanide:furosemide; rare cross-sensitivity with furosemide noted.

items.recommended_action: Flag for awareness — no active orders currently at risk; verify bumetanide IM stock levels as the preferred class substitute and confirm formulary addition pathway with P&T if IM loop diuretic demand arises.

items.summary: Methylprednisolone Acetate Injection (intralesional, preferred formulary) is in active shortage with limited availability as of 02/09/2026, but zero active orders in the past 30 days reduces immediate patient impact.
items.rationale: Rule W3 applies: today_status is Current, yesterday_status is absent (new in snapshot), and no higher-priority rule triggers. Rule C1 and C4 do not apply because active_orders_30d = 0. Rule W1 does not apply because active_orders_30d is 0 (outside the 1–20 range). Default would yield Watch/low, but W3 is the closest match (ongoing Current shortage, no escalating factors). Severity is Watch. Confidence is capped at medium because yesterday_status is absent (non-canonical/missing), active_orders_30d = 0 (reducing urgency but also removing certainty about future demand), and the top-1 alternative label returned is an OTC topical hydrocortisone product, not a parenteral intralesional formulation — limiting clinical equivalence confidence.

alternatives.name: hydrocortisone
alternatives.rationale: Corticosteroid class member; hydrocortisone acetate injectable suspension is used intralesionally for dermatologic conditions similar to methylprednisolone acetate (e.g., keloids, psoriatic plaques). No shortage record found for RxCUI 5492. However, the label data returned for this RxCUI describes an OTC topical product, not a parenteral formulation — a parenteral hydrocortisone acetate product would need to be verified on formulary before substitution. Clinical equivalence at appropriate dose is plausible but requires pharmacist confirmation of available formulation.

alternatives.name: methylprednisolone
alternatives.rationale: Same active moiety as the shortage drug; a different salt or formulation (e.g., methylprednisolone sodium succinate) may be available, but is not equivalent for depot intralesional use. No shortage record found for RxCUI 6902. Formulary status and available formulation must be verified before use as intralesional substitute.

alternatives.name: prednisolone
alternatives.rationale: Corticosteroid class member; prednisolone acetate injectable has been used intralesionally. Shortage status unverified (ranked 3rd, not checked per protocol). Formulary status unknown.

citations.claim: Methylprednisolone Acetate Injection RxCUI 1743855 is in Current shortage status with Limited Availability as of update date 02/09/2026; shortage reason listed as Other.

Methylprednisolone acetate injectable suspension is indicated for intralesional use in alopecia areata, discoid lupus erythematosus, keloids, granuloma annulare, lichen planus, lichen simplex chronicus, psoriatic plaques, and necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum; intralesional dose is 20–60 mg per lesion.

Methylprednisolone acetate injectable suspension is contraindicated for intrathecal administration and in systemic fungal infections (except intra-articular use for localized joint conditions); serious neurologic adverse events have been reported with epidural corticosteroid administration.

Label data returned for hydrocortisone RxCUI 5492 describes an OTC topical formulation only; parenteral intralesional hydrocortisone formulation equivalence cannot be confirmed from this label record.

items.recommended_action: Monitor stock levels for methylprednisolone acetate intralesional injection; with zero active orders no immediate patient impact exists, but notify dermatology and any specialist users of limited availability and initiate formulary review for a parenteral corticosteroid alternative (e.g., intralesional triamcinolone acetonide or hydrocortisone acetate injection) before orders resume.

items.summary: Bupivacaine HCl/Epinephrine Injection (epidural, restricted formulary) entered a new active FDA shortage as of 04/22/2026 with no estimated resolution; zero active orders in the past 30 days limits immediate patient impact, but epidural anesthesia use warrants monitoring.
items.rationale: Rule W3 applies: today_status=Current, yesterday_status absent (new in snapshot), no higher rule triggered. Formulary status is 'restricted' (not 'preferred'), active_orders_30d=0, and no departments are recorded — rules C1, C2, C3, C4 do not fire. W1 requires active_orders_30d between 1–20; W2 requires non-preferred status; neither matches exactly, so W3 (ongoing shortage, no rule above triggered) sets severity to Watch. Active orders count of zero prevents upgrade to Critical, but the epidural route for anesthesia/analgesia in surgical and obstetrical procedures warrants ongoing vigilance. Confidence is capped at medium per rule: active_orders_30d is known (=0, not missing) but all alternatives are class-members only (max medium ceiling applies).

alternatives.name: bupivacaine
alternatives.rationale: Same active moiety (bupivacaine HCl without epinephrine); preservative-free single-dose vials are labeled for lumbar epidural block at 0.25%, 0.5%, and 0.75% concentrations per FDA label. Shortage check returned no active shortage record for RxCUI 1815. Epinephrine component would be lost — clinician must assess whether vasoconstrictor is clinically required for the specific block.

alternatives.name: bupivacaine / meloxicam
alternatives.rationale: Class member containing bupivacaine; indicated for soft-tissue infiltration analgesia, not epidural block — route/indication match is incomplete. Shortage check returned no active shortage record for RxCUI 2560310. Shortage status unverified for formulary fit; clinical equivalence for epidural use is not established.

alternatives.name: ropivacaine
alternatives.rationale: Amide local anesthetic in the same class with established epidural use; widely used as a bupivacaine epidural substitute with a similar sensory/motor profile. Shortage status unverified (ranked 3+, not checked per protocol).

alternatives.name: lidocaine
alternatives.rationale: Amide local anesthetic with labeled epidural indication (1% preservative-free solution for lumbar epidural anesthesia per FDA label). Shorter duration than bupivacaine; appropriate for procedures not requiring prolonged block. Shortage status unverified (ranked 3+, not checked per protocol).

alternatives.name: levobupivacaine
alternatives.rationale: S-enantiomer of bupivacaine; established epidural anesthesia agent with potentially reduced cardiotoxicity. Class-member only — shortage status unverified (ranked 3+, not checked per protocol).

citations.claim: Bupivacaine Hydrochloride, Epinephrine Bitartrate Injection shortage status=Current, availability=Unavailable, reason=Other, updated 04/22/2026; RxCUI 1725082 confirmed in shortage record list

Bupivacaine HCl Injection indicated for lumbar epidural block at 0.25%, 0.5%, 0.75% concentrations; preservative-free single-dose vials required for epidural/caudal use; contraindicated for intrathecal use and obstetrical paracervical block
Dosage and administration: incremental epidural dosing 3–5 mL with aspiration checks required; total daily dose not to exceed 400 mg bupivacaine HCl in 24 hours
Lidocaine HCl injection labeled for lumbar and caudal epidural blocks (1% preservative-free 30 mL single-dose vials); supports lidocaine as epidural alternative

Therapeutic alternatives (bupivacaine, bupivacaine/meloxicam, levobupivacaine, ropivacaine, lidocaine, mepivacaine, dibucaine, etidocaine, articaine, prilocaine) returned as class members for RxCUI 1725082

items.recommended_action: Confirm current epidural bupivacaine/epinephrine inventory levels with pharmacy and anesthesia; alert anesthesia staff to the active shortage and verify that preservative-free plain bupivacaine HCl or ropivacaine in appropriate concentrations is available as an epidural substitute before elective surgical cases are scheduled.

items.summary: Bumetanide Injection (IM) is in active shortage as of 04/02/2026; this drug is non-formulary but has 55 active orders in 30 days across Oncology and ICU, requiring immediate alternative identification.
items.rationale: Rule W2 matches: today_status=Current, formulary_status=non-formulary, active_orders_30d=55 (>0). Although active order volume (55) and critical departments (Oncology, ICU) are high-acuity signals, the non-formulary status governs severity under the rubric. Upgrade to Critical was considered given IM route in ICU/Oncology (Rule C2 criteria), but Rule C2 requires preferred_alternatives to be empty after shortage cross-check — no preferred alternatives are listed, however formulary_status is non-formulary (not preferred), so C2 does not apply. W2 is the first matching rule. Confidence is capped at medium: all alternatives are class-members only (ATC class, no clinical equivalence proof per PRD §13.1 ceiling). The drug is non-formulary yet showing 55 orders — pharmacy should verify whether this reflects off-formulary procurement or a documentation gap.

alternatives.name: bumetanide (oral tablets)
alternatives.rationale: Same active moiety as the injectable form. FDA label confirms nearly equal diuretic response after oral vs. parenteral administration of bumetanide; oral route is appropriate when GI absorption is intact. No current shortage record found for RxCUI 1808. Route switch from IM to PO requires clinical assessment of GI absorption adequacy.

alternatives.name: furosemide
alternatives.rationale: Loop diuretic in the same pharmacological class; bumetanide label notes lack of cross-sensitivity in furosemide-allergic patients and provides a 1:40 conversion ratio (bumetanide:furosemide). Injectable and oral furosemide are widely available. Shortage status unverified (not in top-2 checked). Confirm furosemide formulary status and shortage status before substituting.

alternatives.name: torsemide
alternatives.rationale: Loop diuretic, same ATC class as bumetanide; available in oral and IV formulations. Shortage status unverified (ranked 3+, not checked per call budget). Confirm availability and formulary status before substituting.

citations.claim: Bumetanide Injection shortage status is Current as of update date 04/02/2026; RxCUIs 282486 and 1727569 confirmed in record.

Bumetanide Injection is indicated for edema associated with congestive heart failure, hepatic and renal disease; IM and IV routes are indicated when oral administration is not practical; contraindicated in anuria and hepatic coma.
FDA label states almost equal diuretic response occurs after oral and parenteral administration of bumetanide, supporting oral substitution when GI absorption is intact.
Bumetanide oral tablet label confirms 1:40 bumetanide-to-furosemide substitution ratio and near-equivalent oral/parenteral diuretic response; supports oral route switch and furosemide as alternative.

items.recommended_action: Review the 55 active orders to confirm clinical need for IM route; evaluate switching eligible patients to oral bumetanide or IV/oral furosemide, and verify furosemide and torsemide formulary and shortage status before implementing substitutions.

items.summary: Dextrose 5% Injection (non-preferred, IV) is newly appearing in an active FDA shortage as of 04/23/2026, with zero active orders recorded in the last 30 days at this hospital.
items.rationale: Rule W2 applies: today_status is Current, formulary_status is non-preferred, and active_orders_30d is 0. Although Rule W2 technically requires active_orders_30d > 0, no higher-severity rule matches (C1/C2 require preferred status or critical-care orders; C3 requires a prior resolved status; C4 requires preferred status and active orders). Default Watch classification is applied per Rule W3/default given the ongoing Current status with no prior snapshot entry. Active orders count is 0, so immediate patient impact is low; however, as a basic IV fluid with broad hospital utility, procurement monitoring is warranted. Confidence is capped at medium because active_orders_30d is 0 (effectively unknown impact) and the top-1 alternative label data returned is a consumer oral glucose product — not clinically equivalent to IV dextrose — making alternative validation unreliable.

alternatives.name: glucose
alternatives.rationale: RxNorm class-member alternative. No active FDA shortage record found. However, the openFDA label retrieved for RxCUI 4850 describes an oral anti-nausea consumer product, not an IV dextrose preparation — clinical equivalence as an IV substitute is NOT confirmed by available label data. Manual verification of IV glucose product availability required.

alternatives.name: glycine
alternatives.rationale: RxNorm class-member alternative. No active FDA shortage record found. Glycine IV solutions have limited and specialized indications (e.g., irrigant); not a standard substitute for D5W for hydration/caloric support. Clinical equivalence for D5W indication is not established by available data.

alternatives.name: mannitol
alternatives.rationale: RxNorm class-member alternative; shortage status unverified (ranked 3+, not checked). Mannitol IV is an osmotic agent with distinct indications (cerebral edema, ICP reduction); not clinically equivalent to D5W for general hydration/caloric use. Not recommended as a substitute without clinical re-evaluation.

alternatives.name: sorbitol
alternatives.rationale: RxNorm class-member alternative; shortage status unverified (ranked 3+, not checked). Sorbitol IV is not a standard substitute for D5W in US practice. Clinical equivalence not established.

citations.claim: Dextrose Monohydrate 5% Injection status is Current as of 04/23/2026; RxCUI 1795610 is in the confirmed shortage RxCUI list.

Dextrose 5% Injection indicated as a source of calories and water for hydration in adults and pediatric patients; contraindicated in hypersensitivity to corn products; warnings include fluid overload, hypokalemia, and aluminum toxicity risk.
Label retrieved for RxCUI 4850 (glucose) describes an oral anti-nausea consumer product, not an IV dextrose preparation; clinical equivalence as IV substitute not supported by this label.
Therapeutic alternatives glucose (4850), glycine (4919), mannitol (6628), sorbitol (9945) identified as RxNorm class members for Dextrose 5% Injection.

items.recommended_action: Monitor procurement for Dextrose 5% Injection; with zero current active orders, immediate patient risk is low, but pharmacy should verify IV-formulation availability of any alternative before substitution, as RxNorm class-member alternatives do not confirm clinical equivalence for this indication.
